Midterm Project - GitHub

JSC370 (Winter 2026)

Github https://github.com/Richard098789/JSC370/tree/main/final_project

Introduction

Online job postings provide valuable insights into labour market demand and skills. As a data science student currently looking for a job, I am interested in how do role type, location, and job characteristics relate to salary levels in Canadian data-related job postings? Does salary increase over time?.

In this project, job posting data were collected using the Adzuna Job Search API, which collects job listings from multiple job boards and company career pages. Each job posting contains information such as the job title, company name, location, salary range, contract type, and description. These attributes make the dataset suitable for exploratory data analysis, as it includes a mix of numerical, binary and categorical variables.

The goal of this project is to explore patterns in salary levels across different job characteristics. In particular, the analysis focuses on comparing salaries across major data-related roles such as data scientists, data engineers, and data analysts, as well as examining geographic differences across Canadian regions. Additional exploratory analysis investigates whether the of job posting date is associated with variations in salary levels.

Method

1. Data Collection

  • Use Adzuna Job Search API to collect job postings data. Restrict the search range to Canada and the job title to include “data”. Convert the data into a pandas dataframe. Fetch the first num_pages pages of data, with 50 jobs per page.

  • Collected 3750 job postings, with features: title, company, location, salary_min, salary_max, contract_type, category, created.

2. Data Cleaning

  1. Check and remove missing values.

  2. Remove parentheses and punctuation from the job title.

  3. Filter the data to only include the job titles that contain the keywords “engineer”, “scientist”, “analyst”, or “architect”.

  4. Classify the job titles into four categories: Data Scientist, Data Analyst, Data Engineer, and Other.

  5. Calculate the median salary for each role.

  6. map the contract type to a binary variable: 1 for permanent and 0 for contract.

  7. Clean the location column. Add a location map to convert the location to province.

  8. Categorize the salary into low, medium, and high.

  9. Convert the created date to datetime.

Preliminary Results

0. Basic Summary Statistics

The cleaned dataset contains 371 job postings related to data-focused roles across 9 Canadian provinces. These postings are grouped into 3 primary job role categories, including Data Scientist, Data Analyst, and Data Engineer. The job postings are collected from 2024-07-30 to 2026-03-13. Most jobs are posted in the past 6 months.

Summary

After cleaning and processing the data, the analysis would focus on several key features including job role, location, contract type and created date.

The exploratory analysis revealed several preliminary patterns in the dataset. First, salary distributions differ across job roles. Positions categorized as Data Engineer and Data Scientist generally have higher salary ranges compared to Data Analyst roles.

Second, the geographic distribution of job postings is uneven across Canada. A large proportion of postings in the dataset are located in Ontario, while smaller numbers appear in provinces such as British Columbia, Quebec, and Alberta. Salary distributions also vary across provinces, although the number of observations in some regions is relatively small. These geographic differences suggest that regional job markets may influence salary levels.

Third, the temporal trend analysis shows that average salary have been increasing over time since 2024, with a slight decrease in 2025. Since most job fetched are from recent time, if more job postings are collected from older time, we can further investigate the trend.

Plan

First, I wll expand the data size by fetching more job postings using Adzuna API to include more job postings from older time. This would help learn more about the trend of salary over time.

Next I will use ML methods to further explore the relationship between salary and other features. As an initial baseline, a linear regression model will be fitted to estimate salary based on features such as job role, geographic location, contract type, and posting date. To capture potential nonlinear relationships, models such as GAM will also be used. Finally, ensemble learning methods including random forests and gradient boosting will be used to improve predictive performance and identify the most important factors influencing salary.

I would focus on exploring role type, and whether different months of a year have different salary levels. I would also focus on exploring region and compare them with actual Canadian geographic boundaries to better understand regional job market patterns.

The result of the model will be added to interactive website, where user can pick the features they are interested in and see the predicted salary.
